---
title: "The Algebra"
---

The codex names three primitives. This chapter types them precisely and states
the laws that make compositions safe.

## Typing the primitives

Let `World` be the live Editor state — open scenes, the entity worlds, the
console, compile status.

```{.right lang="text"}
Inspect : World -> Observation
Mutate  : World -> World
Verify  : World -> Observation -> Bool
```

`Inspect` is a pure projection of the world into an observation. It is
referentially transparent: two consecutive inspects with no intervening mutate
yield the same observation. This is why inspection is always safe to repeat and
always the first move.

`Mutate` is the only primitive that changes the world. It is the only source of
risk, so it carries the heaviest obligation: it must be bracketed by inspection
before and verification after.

`Verify` is total — it is defined for every world and returns a decision, never
a maybe. A verify that cannot decide is not a verify; it is another inspect
wearing a verify's hat.

## The bracket law

::: {.axiom title="Mutation is always bracketed"}
`Inspect ∘ Mutate ∘ Verify`. Any `Mutate` not preceded by an `Inspect` of its
target and followed by a `Verify` of its effect is undefined behavior.
:::

The bracket is not ceremony. Inspection before mutation answers *can this
mutation even apply* — does the target exist, is it in the parent scene or the
subscene, am I in play mode where the structural change is illegal.
Verification after mutation answers *did it apply* — not *did the command
return without error*.

::: {.gotcha title="A returning command is not a verified effect"}
`unity-cli editor refresh --compile` can return `connection closed before
response` while it is still recompiling. The command "completed"; nothing is
verified. Treat it as inconclusive and re-inspect compile state with a minimal
`exec`.
:::

## Idempotence where state must exist

When a mutation creates something that should exist exactly once — a marker
object, a SubScene, a singleton — make the mutation idempotent: inspect for the
existing instance and reuse it, rather than creating a second.

```{.exec name="Idempotent marker creation"}
var scene = UnityEditor.SceneManagement.EditorSceneManager.GetActiveScene();
var roots = scene.GetRootGameObjects();
var i = 0;
while (i < roots.Length) {
    if (roots[i].name == "Marker") return "reused existing Marker";
    i++;
}
var go = new UnityEngine.GameObject("Marker");
return "created Marker";
```

Run it twice; the world ends in the same state. That is the property that lets
you re-run a recipe after a partial failure without accumulating garbage.

## Determinism over convenience

Inspection chooses `while` with an explicit index over `foreach` not from
taste but because the SKILL has observed `foreach` failing inside `exec`. The
deterministic form is the one that always parses, always disposes, always
reads the same way to the next person. When two forms are equivalent, the
codex keeps the one with fewer ways to be wrong.

```{.wrong}
foreach (var e in query.ToEntityArray(Allocator.Temp)) { Use(e); }
```

```{.right}
var arr = query.ToEntityArray(Allocator.Temp);
for (var i = 0; i < arr.Length; i++) { Use(arr[i]); }
arr.Dispose();
```

The right form is longer and that is the point: the allocation is named, owned,
and disposed. Nothing leaks because nothing is implicit.
